timeline.event: Born in Kiel, Holstein.
Witnessed the march of Prussian and Austrian troops into Kiel during the Second Schleswig War.
Family moved to Munich, and he enrolled in the Maximiliansgymnasium.
Began his studies at the University of Munich.
Studied for a year at the Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Defended his doctoral dissertation 'On the Second Law of Thermodynamics'.
Earned his habilitation by presenting the thesis 'Equilibrium States of Isotropic Bodies at Different Temperatures'.
Appointed associate professor of theoretical physics at the University of Kiel.
Married Marie Merck.
Appointed as the successor to Kirchhoff's position at the Friedrich Wilhelm University of Berlin.
Became a full professor.
Turned his attention to the problem of black-body radiation.
Published 'Treatise on Thermodynamics'.
Contributed to the formation of the German Physical Society (DPG).
Presented a new derivation of Planck's law of black-body radiation, including the energy quantization hypothesis.
Served as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) (until 1909).
His first wife, Marie Planck, died.
Married his second wife, Marga von Hoesslin.
Successfully brought Einstein to Berlin and created a new professorship for him.
His eldest son, Karl, was killed in action at the Battle of Verdun.
His daughter, Grete, died while giving birth to her first child.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta (actually awarded in 1919).
His daughter, Emma, died.
Founded the Emergency Association of German Science (Notgemeinschaft der Deutschen Wissenschaft) along with Fritz Haber.
Retired in Berlin, and Erwin Schrödinger succeeded him.
Became president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).
Was 74 years old when the Nazis took power, and discussed the treatment of Jewish professors with Hitler.
His term as president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG) ended.
Celebrated his 80th birthday, and resigned as president of the Prussian Academy when it was taken over by the Nazis.
His home in Berlin was destroyed in a bombing raid, resulting in the loss of all his scientific records and correspondence.
His son Erwin was executed for his involvement in the assassination attempt on Hitler.
Died in Göttingen.
The Kaiser Wilhelm Society was renamed the Max Planck Society (MPG).

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of energy quanta
keyAchievements.description: Won the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, which revolutionized the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of quantum theory
keyAchievements.description: His reputation as a physicist rests primarily on his role as the originator of quantum theory, a revolutionary idea incompatible with classical physics.

keyAchievements.title: Planck's law of black-body radiation
keyAchievements.description: Derived Planck's law of black-body radiation, which accurately described the experimentally observed black-body spectrum, through his research on the black-body radiation problem.

keyAchievements.title: Planck constant (h)
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of quantum theory by introducing the Planck constant (h) through the hypothesis that electromagnetic energy could only be emitted in quantized form.

keyAchievements.title: Early recognition of and contribution to the theory of relativity
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the few to immediately recognize the significance of Albert Einstein's special theory of relativity, and contributed to its reformulation.

keyAchievements.title: Leadership in German scientific institutions
keyAchievements.description: Held key positions in the German scientific community, serving as president of the German Physical Society (DPG) and the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (KWG).

faq.question: What major scientific discovery did Max Planck make?
faq.answer: Max Planck discovered energy quanta and founded quantum theory, revolutionizing the understanding of atomic and subatomic processes.

faq.question: When did Max Planck win the Nobel Prize in Physics?
faq.answer: He was awarded the 1918 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of energy quanta, though he actually received it in 1919.

faq.question: What was Max Planck's stance regarding the Nazi regime?
faq.answer: He tried to assist his Jewish colleagues and preserve the independence of science; he met with Hitler to discuss the treatment of Jewish professors and resigned from the presidency of the Prussian Academy in protest against the Nazis.

faq.question: What were Max Planck's religious views?
faq.answer: He was a German Lutheran who believed in an almighty, omniscient, and benevolent God (though not necessarily a personal one), and viewed both science and religion as having the goal of 'Toward God!'.

timeline.event: Born on June 23
Started majoring in mathematics at the University of Cambridge
Took physicist Eddington's scientific methodology class and proved the central limit theorem
Left for Princeton University after providing a negative proof to the decidability problem
Finished studies at Princeton University and returned to Cambridge
Joined the Government Code and Cypher School (GCCS) during World War II and started cryptography research
Devised the Turing machine; pushed for the creation of a mathematics department to build an electronic calculator at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL) on October 1
Awarded the Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE)
Published a paper titled 'Can Machines Think?' in the philosophy journal 'Mind'
Arrested and convicted by British police on charges of homosexuality
Published a paper on the Riemann zeta function
Died of potassium cyanide poisoning on June 7
Queen Elizabeth II pardoned Turing's homosexuality conviction on December 24

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Turing machine
keyAchievements.description: Formalized the concepts of algorithm and computation through an abstract model called the Turing machine, making a profound contribution to the development of computer science.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Turing test
keyAchievements.description: Proposed an experiment to evaluate how human-like a machine's answers are with the proposition 'Can machines think?', achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Cracked the Enigma code
keyAchievements.description: Successfully cracked the German codes during World War II by introducing a code-breaking machine called the Bombe.

keyAchievements.title: Proved the central limit theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved it in 1933 without knowing that Finnish mathematician Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg had already proved it in 1922.

keyAchievements.title: Negative proof of the decidability problem
keyAchievements.description: Provided a negative proof to the decidability problem in 1936.

faq.question: What fields was Alan Turing a pioneer in?
faq.answer: He was an English computer scientist, mathematician, cryptanalyst, logician, and a pioneering figure in computer science.

faq.question: What is the purpose of the Turing Award?
faq.answer: It is an annual prize given by the ACM to those who have made significant contributions to computer science, established in 1966 to honor Turing's contributions.

faq.question: Why is Alan Turing called the 'father of computer science'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'father of computer science' because he made profound contributions to theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence.

faq.question: When was Alan Turing pardoned for his homosexuality conviction?
faq.answer: Queen Elizabeth II pardoned his homosexuality conviction on December 24, 2013, 59 years after his death.

timeline.event: Born in Afshana near Bukhara as the child of Abdullah ibn Al-Hasan, a tax collector of the Samanid Empire, and Sitara.
At the age of five, his family moved to Bukhara, and he entered a private school in Bukhara.
By the age of ten, he could already recite the Quran and many Arabic literary works from memory.
Started treating patients at the age of sixteen.
Studied on his own at eighteen and came to understand Aristotelian philosophy through Al-Farabi's commentaries.
At the age of eighteen, he wrote his first work, 'The Compendium of Various Sciences', for his neighbor Al-Aruzi.
The Samanid Empire fell due to attacks by the Ghaznavids and the Karakhanids.
His father Abdullah passed away.
At twenty-one, he wrote and presented the 20-volume encyclopedia 'The Book of Fair Judgment' for the jurist Al-Barqi.
Left Bukhara at twenty-two and embarked on a wandering journey.
Was employed by Mamun II, the ruler of Urgench in the Khwarezm region, became an official, and started writing 'The Canon of Medicine'.
Moved to Rey near Tehran and wrote about 30 short essays.
Completed 'The Canon of Medicine', which he had been writing for some time.
After Shams al-Dawla passed away, Ibn Sina abandoned his official post and went into seclusion.
Was imprisoned by Sama al-Dawla, the new ruler of Hamadan.
Isfahan was attacked by Mas'ud I, the ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire, and Ibn Sina's belongings, including his collection of books, were looted.
Died in Hamadan.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of philosophy and medicine
keyAchievements.description: Compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy and medicine, and had a profound impact on European medicine and philosophy by integrating Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Canon of Medicine
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time and was used as a standard medical text in Europe until the 17th century.

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Book of Healing
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'The Book of Healing', an encyclopedia covering all fields of study except ethics and politics.

keyAchievements.title: Natural science research
keyAchievements.description: Denied the possibility of alchemical transmutation of metals, and researched various fields of natural science including mathematics, physics, astronomy, and mineralogy.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European academia
keyAchievements.description: Influenced Thomas Aquinas and became the catalyst for the revival of Aristotelianism in Europe in the late 12th century.

faq.question: How is Ibn Sina evaluated as a figure?
faq.answer: A Persian philosopher, physician, and scientist, he is regarded as one of the greatest medical scholars of the Middle Ages and the finest intellectual produced by the Islamic world.

faq.question: What is Ibn Sina's major medical work?
faq.answer: He authored 'The Canon of Medicine', which compiled all known medical knowledge of the time, and this book was used as the basic textbook of European medicine until the 17th century.

faq.question: What philosophical contributions did Ibn Sina make?
faq.answer: He compiled Greek and Arabic philosophy, integrated Aristotelian philosophy and Neoplatonism, and greatly influenced European medicine and philosophy.

faq.question: What was Ibn Sina's stance on alchemy?
faq.answer: He denied the possibility of metal transmutation believed by alchemists of the time, arguing that the differences between metals are profound and that precious metals created through alchemy are merely imitations.

timeline.event: Born in Paris as the first son of Jean-Antoine Lavoisier and Emilie Lavoisier.
Graduated from College Mazarin.
Obtained a bachelor's degree in law.
Became a member of the French Academy of Sciences and joined the Ferme Generale, a tax collection agency.
Became famous by publishing a paper concluding that water cannot be transmuted into earth.
Married Marie-Anne Pierrette Paulze.
Published 'Opuscules Physiques et chimiques' (Physical and Chemical Essays).
Appointed head of the Gunpowder Administration by Finance Minister Anne Robert Jacques Turgot.
Revealed that water is produced when hydrogen is burned, in cooperation with Pierre-Simon Laplace.
Became a director of the French Academy of Sciences.
Published 'Methode de nomenclature chimique' (Method of Chemical Nomenclature) by reforming chemical nomenclature together with Claude Louis Berthollet and others.
Published 'Traite Elementaire de Chimie' (Elementary Treatise on Chemistry).
Participated in the creation of a unified system of weights and measures commissioned by the National Assembly.
Numerous academies, including the French Academy of Sciences, were closed.
Imprisoned along with his father-in-law Jacques Paulze.
Sentenced to death and beheaded at the guillotine in the Place de la Revolution.
Marie-Anne Lavoisier was officially able to receive Lavoisier's confiscated belongings back.

keyAchievements.title: Led the Chemical Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Led the 18th-century chemical revolution, leaving a mark in the history of chemistry and biology.

keyAchievements.title: Abolished the phlogiston theory and established the theory of combustion
keyAchievements.description: Advanced chemistry significantly by advocating a new theory of combustion, discarding the phlogiston theory, and identifying the role of oxygen.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Law of Conservation of Mass
keyAchievements.description: Established the law of conservation of mass, stating that matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions, but only changes form.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation of modern chemistry by distinguishing between elements and compounds and leading a systematic reform of chemical nomenclature.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced quantitative methods and compiled a list of elements
keyAchievements.description: Introduced quantitative methods to chemistry for the first time, compiled the first extensive list of elements, and predicted the existence of silicon.

faq.question: For what reason was Antoine Lavoisier executed?
faq.answer: During the height of the French Revolution, he was indicted for tax fraud and selling adulterated tobacco, and despite his contributions to science, he was executed by the guillotine.

faq.question: What is Lavoisier's most important scientific achievement?
faq.answer: He led the chemical revolution by discarding the phlogiston theory and identifying the role of oxygen in combustion and respiration, established the law of conservation of mass, and laid the foundation for modern chemical nomenclature.

faq.question: How did Lavoisier's wife, Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier, contribute to her husband's research?
faq.answer: As a laboratory assistant, Marie-Anne helped by drawing laboratory equipment, recording results, translating English papers, and she also participated in the development of the metric measurement system.

timeline.event: Born on July 3 in the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty
Began the work of compiling standard medical texts at the age of 35
Died in the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty (at the age of 76)
Publication of the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (after Li Shizhen's death)

keyAchievements.title: Authored the Compendium of Materia Medica
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Compendium of Materia Medica' (Bencao Gangmu), which integrated herbal medicine and had a profound influence on subsequent Eastern medicine and natural history.

keyAchievements.title: Contributed to acupuncture and diagnostics
keyAchievements.description: Also contributed to the fields of acupuncture and diagnostics.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Binhu Maixue
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Binhu Maixue', a book on pulse diagnosis.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Qijing Bamai Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Qijing Bamai Kao', a book on the eight extraordinary meridians.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Wuzang Tulun
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Wuzang Tulun', a book on the five internal organs, which is currently lost.

keyAchievements.title: Authored Mingmen Kao
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Mingmen Kao', a book on the 'Gate of Life' (Mingmen), which is currently lost.

faq.question: When was Li Shizhen born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen was born on July 3, 1518, the 13th year of Zhengde of the Ming Dynasty, and died in 1593, the 21st year of Wanli of the Ming Dynasty, at the age of 76.

faq.question: What are Li Shizhen's major works?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's major work is the 'Compendium of Materia Medica', which integrated herbal medicine. In addition, he authored 'Binhu Maixue', 'Qijing Bamai Kao', and others.

faq.question: What positions did Li Shizhen hold?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen served as a physician for the Prince of Chu, became an imperial physician in the royal court, and later held the position of magistrate of Huanggang, Hubei in the Ming Dynasty.

faq.question: What was Li Shizhen's family background?
faq.answer: Li Shizhen's grandfather and father were doctors. His father's name was Li Yanwen, his courtesy name was Ziyu, and his pseudonym was Yuechi.
